summary: Gruber explores why iPhones still use numbered names while most other Apple products don't. After polling Twitter, where 70% predicted 'iPhone 13,' he examines why many wish Apple would drop numbers in favor of model-year naming like iPads and Macs. The key difference is that Apple continues selling older iPhone models at lower prices for years, unlike iPads which are replaced outright. Naming an older model 'iPhone (2018)' would make it feel dated, undermining Apple's strategy of positioning previous-generation iPhones as attractive new purchases. He concludes the numbering scheme will likely persist despite its inconsistencies.
key_points: Unlike most Apple products, older iPhone models remain on sale for years at reduced prices, which makes year-based naming impractical
Apple wants buyers of 2-3 year old iPhones to feel like they're getting a new product, not a dated one — model years would undermine that
Apple Watch is the only other product with incremental numbering, because it follows the same older-models-stay-on-sale pattern
The iPhone numbering scheme is historically messy — no iPhone 2, no iPhone 9, and 'S' years disrupted the count so badly that the 15th iPhone generation is called iPhone 13
money_quotes: Apple wants people who are buying new iPhones that were first introduced 2–3 years ago to feel like they're getting a new iPhone.
But if the iPhone XR were named 'iPhone (2018)', it'd feel old.
I get it: it seems odd that in 10 years we might be awaiting the introduction of the iPhone 23 lineup, but at the moment, I don't see this changing.
The NFL just keeps counting Super Bowls — at least Apple only used Roman numerals for the X and XS/XR years.
When Apple introduces a new iPad Pro, it replaces the previous iPad Pro. You can't go into an Apple Store and buy a new 2018 iPad Pro. But you can buy a new iPhone XR from Apple today.
If Apple had stuck to a numbering scheme as simple as Apple Watch's, next week's new iPhones would be the iPhones 15.
key_insight: iPhones keep numbered names because Apple sells older models for years at lower prices, and year-based naming would make them feel outdated rather than like appealing new purchases.
